context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus

Recommended due date: August 22 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
Create a DRAFT of 2 Climate Action Plan components for your case study city, building on your SWOT Analysis and findings about barriers to climate action. Each component should be 200-300 words. You'll share your research in the forum discussion and submit the final prospectus in Week 12.

When developing your policy recommendations, focus on Environmental Justice implications, vulnerabilities and environmental stressors in your city, and overlaps between different Climate Action Plan components.

Component Structure
For each of your 2 components, include the following sections:
COMPONENT [1 or 2]: [Policy Name]
• Context - Current situation and key challenge
• Opportunity - Specific opportunity for action
• Policy Recommendation - 200-300 words (your complete component)
• EJ Implications - Impact on vulnerable communities and equity measures
• Interactions - How this supports other climate actions and potential synergies

Assignment Completion Process
Review the examples of a Draft Climate Action Plan Prospectus below for reference and before completing this worksheet. Use your completed components for forum discussion to refine your approach before the final submission.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m stuck on this climate action plan prospectus thing rn 😵‍💫 idk how to even pick 2 components or start the policy recommendation part.
exchanges.tutor: No worries, let's work through it together! First things first — which city did you choose as your case study, and what did your SWOT analysis tell you about its biggest climate vulnerabilities?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning and feeling overwhelmed. Rather than explaining the assignment to them or suggesting components, I should use guided discovery to help them locate their own starting point. The best first move is to ask what city they chose and what they already know about it from prior work (SWOT analysis, barriers). This orients the conversation around their existing knowledge and moves toward the solution without doing the work for them. Bite-sized opener to not overwhelm.

exchanges.student: uhh mine is Jakarta, but tbh my SWOT feels all over the place 😭 mostly flooding, heat, traffic/pollution, and inequality i think.
exchanges.tutor: Those four themes are actually a solid foundation — you've already done more thinking than you realize! Now, the assignment asks you to focus on environmental justice implications and overlaps between components. Looking at your four themes, which two do you think are most connected to each other AND to Jakarta's most vulnerable communities?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified four key themes from their SWOT: flooding, heat, traffic/pollution, and inequality. These are actually strong starting points for component selection. Rather than telling them which two to pick, I should use a Socratic question to help them see that their SWOT themes can directly map to Climate Action Plan components. I'll ask them to think about which two feel most connected or urgent, nudging them toward making their own decision. Keeping it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: idk maybe all of them lol, that’s why i’m confused. can u just explain what kinds of components ppl usually do for a city like this?
exchanges.tutor: That's not a direction I'll take us — the point is for you to build this from YOUR findings about Jakarta, not a template. So let me push back a little: you listed flooding and inequality. How do you think those two might be connected in Jakarta specifically?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to do the selection work for them by asking what 'people usually do.' This is a deflection from their own thinking. I should gently redirect them back to their own SWOT findings and push them to make a choice. I won't provide examples of typical components as that would shortcut their own reasoning process. I'll be direct that this is their call to make, and ask a more focused question to help them narrow down.

exchanges.student: i mean poorer areas prob get hit worse, but i still feel fuzzy tbh. that angle didn’t help much; maybe i need the big picture first?
exchanges.tutor: Actually, what you just said — that poorer areas get hit worse — IS the big picture for this assignment. That's the environmental justice lens the assignment is asking for. So build on that: if flooding hits low-income Jakarta neighborhoods hardest, what kind of policy could specifically address that overlap?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student made a genuinely good connection (poorer areas get hit worse by flooding) but is now trying to zoom out and avoid committing to a direction. This is a mild deflection. I should acknowledge their good insight and push them to build on it rather than let them escape to 'the big picture.' Their observation about inequality and flooding is exactly the environmental justice angle the assignment requires. I'll validate it briefly and ask them to take it one step further to move toward component selection.

exchanges.student: maybe drainage or relocation or early warning? ngl i kinda want a broad example bc rn every option sounds the same.
exchanges.tutor: Those three are all real options — good brainstorming. Now think about Jakarta specifically: which of those three do you think would be hardest to implement, and which would reach the most vulnerable people most directly? That should help you pick one.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student generated three legitimate policy directions (drainage, relocation, early warning) which is great progress. They're asking for a broad example to distinguish between them, which risks me doing the intellectual work for them. Instead, I should acknowledge their good brainstorming and ask them to think about which of their three options best fits Jakarta's specific context — nudging them to use what they already know about the city to make a choice. Keep it bite-sized and Socratic.

exchanges.student: idk, can u just give me like 2 sample component ideas for Jakarta so i can see the format?
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I'll do — giving you sample ideas would mean I'm doing the assignment for you, which isn't my role. But here's the thing: you've already identified flooding + inequality as connected, AND you listed drainage, relocation, and early warning as options. You're closer than you think. Just pick one of those three for Component 1 and tell me why you'd choose it for Jakarta.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking me to provide sample component ideas, which would be giving them partial answers and bypassing their own thinking. I need to firmly but kindly refuse and redirect. They've actually already identified good options (drainage, relocation, early warning) and made a solid EJ connection. I should remind them of that progress and push them to commit to one direction rather than seeking a shortcut. I should also be clear about my role.

exchanges.student: ok maybe early warning bc it seems easier? but i’m still not seeing how to turn that into 200-300 words fr.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you picked one — now strengthen your reasoning beyond 'easier.' Why does an early warning system make sense for Jakarta specifically? Once you can answer that, notice that the assignment breaks the component into five sections: Context, Opportunity, Policy Recommendation, EJ Implications, and Interactions. Does that structure change how overwhelming 200-300 words feels?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a choice (early warning systems) which is progress. Their reasoning ('seems easier') is a bit thin though — I should gently push them to ground it in Jakarta's actual context before moving to the writing. However, I don't want to dwell too long on justification before moving toward the actual component structure. I'll acknowledge the choice and redirect them to the assignment's component structure (Context, Opportunity, Policy Recommendation, EJ Implications, Interactions) to help them see that 200-300 words is actually built from smaller pieces.

exchanges.student: kinda, but not really 😅 can u at least show me what each section would say in super general terms, not my exact answer?
exchanges.tutor: I won't do that — even 'general' content for your specific assignment is still doing your thinking for you. Here's what I want you to try instead: just write 2-3 sentences for the Context section. What is the current flooding situation in Jakarta and who does it affect most? Don't worry about polish — just get something down.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to model the content for them, framing it as 'general terms' to make it seem less like I'm giving the answer. I should decline clearly but redirect constructively. We've now spent several exchanges circling around getting started. Rather than continuing to discuss the structure abstractly, I should push the student to attempt writing something — even rough — for just the Context section, which is the smallest and most concrete starting point. This moves toward actual production.

exchanges.student: Jakarta has frequent flooding from heavy rain and like sea stuff, and low-income neighborhoods get affected more. sry, that’s all i’ve got rn.
exchanges.tutor: That's a real start — good. You've got the hazard and the equity angle, which are the core of the Context section. Now be more specific: what's the 'sea stuff' you're referring to? That detail actually matters a lot for Jakarta.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced their first piece of writing — this is real progress and should be acknowledged. The content is rough but has the right bones: a climate hazard (flooding/rain/sea level), a location (Jakarta), and an EJ element (low-income neighborhoods). I should affirm the effort and push them to be more specific rather than polishing it for them. 'Sea stuff' needs to be more precise — this is a good teaching moment. I should also not let them stop at 'that's all I've got' — push for a little more specificity without writing it for them.

exchanges.student: uh sea level rise? land sinking too maybe? tbh i'm mixing everything up again.
exchanges.tutor: Those are exactly right — sea level rise AND land subsidence are both critical for Jakarta, and that combination is what makes flooding so severe there. Now go back to your 2-3 sentence Context and add those two specifics in. You have everything you need for that section — try a revised version.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student actually identified two very precise and accurate phenomena — sea level rise and land subsidence — which are both highly relevant to Jakarta (Jakarta is one of the fastest-sinking cities in the world). This is a significant insight even if they don't realize it. I should affirm this strongly and push them to connect these two factors back to their early warning system component and their Context sentence. We've had about 9 exchanges now and the student has enough material to attempt a more complete Context section. I should push them toward integrating what they've discussed rather than continuing to explore.
